// SLIDE 01 — HOOK

CHAPTER 11 — PLANNING YOUR FIRST BOONDOGGLED BUILD

The planning sequence has five documents and four gates between them. The documents are evidence the gates were honored.

Chapter 11 — Planning Your First Boondog — /v0 is one sentence. One user. One outcome. No conjunctions hiding a second project. "An application tracker that's also
NARRATION

The planning sequence has five documents and four gates between them. The documents are evidence the gates were honored. The gates are the actual discipline. /v0 is one sentence. One user. One outcome. No conjunctions hiding a second project. "An application tracker that's also a scholarship database and an essay editor" is three projects wearing one sentence as a disguise.

// SLIDE 02 — SCOPE

SCOPE

A first Boondoggled build should be smaller than the project you think you want to do. This is the most reliable advice

Scope — The reason is mechanical. Boondoggling has per-step overhead that ungoverned vibe-coding does not have. If you choose a
NARRATION

A first Boondoggled build should be smaller than the project you think you want to do. This is the most reliable advice in this book and the advice students most reliably ignore. The reason is mechanical. Boondoggling has per-step overhead that ungoverned vibe-coding does not have. If you choose a project at the upper limit of what you could build without the discipline, you will hit the overhead and quit.

// SLIDE 03 — WHAT THE SCORE TELLS YOU BEFOR

WHAT THE SCORE TELLS YOU BEFORE YOU WRITE A LINE.

1 — Phase 1 — Data model
2 — Phase 1 — Page scaffold
3 — Phase 1 — Mutations
4 — Phase 1 — Visual hierarchy
NARRATION

The Boondoggle Score is not a checklist. It is a diagnostic. A honestly produced score tells you three things by inspection, before you have touched the keyboard in anger. The critical path is the sequence of rows where each handoff condition is a precondition for the next row's labor.

// SLIDE 04 — WHEN THE SCORE CATCHES A THIN

WHEN THE SCORE CATCHES A THIN SDD SECTION.

The most common early diagnostic, and the one that feels like failure the first time: you finish the SDD, start writing

When the Score Catches a Thin SDD Sectio — The Score is telling you a section of the SDD is thin. "The function works" is what you write when the failure modes sec
NARRATION

The most common early diagnostic, and the one that feels like failure the first time: you finish the SDD, start writing the Score, and at some row you cannot write a clean handoff condition. The condition you write keeps being vague. "The function works." "The data is saved." "The page looks right." You can hear, in those words, that you do not know what works means for this row. The Score is telling you a section of the SDD is thin.

// SLIDE 05 — THE PLANNING GATE

THE PLANNING GATE.

The planning gate is the moment you decide planning is complete and the first prompt is permitted. It is the most import

The Planning Gate — The gate opens when all four sub-gates are satisfied. The informal fifth test is this: you have not thought about Claude
NARRATION

The planning gate is the moment you decide planning is complete and the first prompt is permitted. It is the most important decision in the build, and almost every student gets it wrong in the same direction: they open it early, because planning is slow and execution is fast, and the asymmetry is uncomfortable. The gate opens when all four sub-gates are satisfied. The informal fifth test is this: you have not thought about Claude for the last ten minutes.

// SLIDE 06 — THE APPLICATION TRACKER PLAN

THE APPLICATION TRACKER PLAN.

1 — Phase 1
2 — Phase 1
3 — Phase 1
4 — Phase 1
NARRATION

Here is the complete planning document for the build executed in Chapter 12. It fits on roughly one printed page. Two hours to write, most of them spent on the out-of-scope list and the failure modes , the two sections students most want to skip, and which catch the most bugs. A web page that lets me add, mark submitted, and delete college applications for this admissions cycle, and remembers them across browser refreshes.

// SLIDE 07 — 🕰️ AI WAYBACK MACHINE

🕰️ AI WAYBACK MACHINE.

Grace Hopper (1936–2022). British-Austrian-American architect and design theorist. A Pattern Language (Oxford University

🕰️ AI Wayback Machine — Grace Hopper, 1980s. AI-generated portrait based on a public domain photograph (Wikimedia Commons).
NARRATION

Grace Hopper (1936, 2022). British-Austrian-American architect and design theorist. A Pattern Language (Oxford University Press, 1977), co-authored with Sara Ishikawa and Murray Silverstein, presented 253 design patterns, each structured in the same three movements: problem statement, discussion, core solution. The book physically refuses to present a solution until the problem has been named in standalone form. The Timeless Way of Building (1979) is the theoretical companion: design begins with what the inhabitant needs, not with what the builder wants to make.

// SLIDE 08 — THESIS

NARRATION

Grace Hopper (1936, 2022). British-Austrian-American architect and design theorist. A Pattern Language (Oxford University Press, 1977), co-authored with Sara Ishikawa and Murray Silverstein, presented 253 design patterns, each structured in the same three movements: problem statement, discussion, core solution. The book physically refuses to present a solution until the problem has been named in standalone form. The Timeless Way of Building (1979) is the theoretical companion: design begins with what the inhabitant needs, not with what the builder wants to make.
